title: Cat Overgrooming Belly Until Bald: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat licking their belly hair off? Learn why cats overgroom, when to see a vet, and how a photo helps triage this common feline issue.
content_html:
What is Cat Overgrooming?

When a cat licks, bites, or pulls their fur until a bald patch appears—most commonly on the belly—it is known as overgrooming. In the veterinary world, this is often called psychogenic alopecia when caused by stress, or it may be a symptom of pruritus (itchiness). It indicates that your cat is experiencing either physical discomfort, such as an allergy or pain, or emotional distress.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for a cat overgrooming their belly is generally Low. This is typically a chronic issue rather than an acute crisis. However, if the skin looks raw, is bleeding, has a foul odor, or if your cat is also lethargic and refusing food, you should schedule a veterinary appointment more promptly. While not an emergency, the underlying cause needs to be addressed to prevent secondary skin infections.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a clear, high-resolution photo of the affected area is a vital step in the triage process. A photo allows a veterinary professional to see the exact pattern of hair loss and the condition of the skin—checking for redness, bumps, or crusting—without the cat being further stressed by a physical examination. This visual evidence helps determine if the issue is likely behavioral, parasitic, or allergic in nature.

faqs.question: Could my cat have fleas even if I don't see them?
faqs.answer: Yes. Many cats are highly allergic to flea saliva. Even a single flea bite can trigger intense grooming, and cats are so efficient at grooming that they often remove the evidence of fleas before you can see them.

faqs.question: Can stress alone cause a cat to go bald on their stomach?
faqs.answer: Yes, this is known as psychogenic alopecia. Changes in the household, such as a new pet, moving, or a change in routine, can cause a cat to groom compulsively as a way to self-soothe.

faqs.question: Will the hair grow back?
faqs.answer: In most cases, yes. Once the underlying cause—whether it be allergies, parasites, or stress—is identified and treated, the hair follicles will usually recover and the fur will return.
